title: Tropical Subrepresentations and Matroids
speaker: Jaiung Jun
abstract:
In their recent paper, Giansiracusa and Manaker introduced a notion of tropical subrepresentations of linear representations by considering linear actions on tropical linear spaces. In particular, this framework naturally brings matroids into the picture. In this talk, I will present several elementary results on tropical subrepresentations.
This is joint work with Kalina Mincheva and Jeffrey Tolliver.

proof_stages.label: Tropical semiring and combinatorial vector-space background
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up tropical and Boolean semifields, explains how semiring tropical geometry leads to restrictive free-module linear algebra, and motivates three possible replacements for vector spaces: free tropical modules, valuated matroids, and finite pointed sets.

proof_stages.label: Naive free tropical representations reduce to finite G-sets
proof_stages.summary: For representations on T^n, the tropical general linear group is monomial, so a representation determines an action on basis lines and indecomposable representations of a finite group correspond to transitive finite G-sets, with irreducibles collapsing to one-dimensional objects.

proof_stages.label: Tropical subrepresentations as invariant matroids
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing valuated matroids and tropical linear spaces, the speaker recalls the Giansiracusa–Manaker definition of tropical subrepresentation and explains that over the Boolean semifield it is exactly a group action by automorphisms of a matroid, especially for the regular representation.

proof_stages.label: Rank-two invariant matroids via equivalence relations
proof_stages.summary: For a finite G-set X, loopless rank-two matroids are encoded by the relation that two elements form a circuit, yielding a bijection between G-invariant loopless rank-two matroids and nontrivial G-invariant equivalence relations on X, recovering the Marcus–Phillips proper-subgroup classification for X=G.

proof_stages.label: Rank-three classification by simplification and coset relations
proof_stages.summary: Rank-three G-invariant matroids are classified by first passing equivariantly to their simplification and then encoding simple rank-three matroids on cosets G/H by suitable nontrivial equivalence relations on G/H minus one point satisfying stated compatibility conditions.

proof_stages.label: Abelian specialization through distinct difference systems
proof_stages.summary: In finite abelian groups, distinct difference systems are introduced to package the equivalence-relation data, producing simple invariant matroids and giving all simple rank-three invariant matroids for cyclic groups of prime order.

proof_stages.label: Weak automorphisms of valuated matroids and weak G-actions
proof_stages.summary: The Boolean invariant-matroid picture is extended to valuated matroids by defining weak automorphisms as permutations compatible with basis weights up to tropical scalars, relating them to automorphisms of the tropical linear space via exact sequences and a coordinate-semiring construction, and proving that tropical subrepresentations correspond to weak G-actions up to conjugacy.

examples.name: Tropical line from x + y + 1
examples.note: The polynomial x+y+1 is tropicalized to show concretely how a tropical locus and the Giansiracusa-style congruence/coordinate semiring arise.
examples.engagement_reason: he tropicalizes the specific polynomial x+y+1, identifies the locus where the maximum is attained twice, and describes the associated congruence relations

examples.name: Quiver representations over F1
examples.note: The speaker recalls quiver representations over finite pointed sets and their subrepresentations as down-closed subgraphs to motivate the broader theme of replacing vector spaces by combinatorial objects.
examples.engagement_reason: he describes quiver representations over F1 as background and states a general counting theorem, but does not run a concrete quiver example

passes.reason: Quiver representations over F1 replace vector spaces by finite pointed sets, retaining less linear structure than ordinary quiver representations.
Quiver representations over F1 replace vector spaces by finite pointed sets, a minimal less-structured analogue used as motivation.
quiver representations over F1 replace vector spaces by finite pointed sets, giving a less-structured field-one analogue used for motivation.

over F1 the representation replaces vector spaces by finite pointed sets with minimal additive structure, serving as motivation rather than a failure case
F1 quiver representations replace vector spaces by finite pointed sets, a limiting minimal-base version of linear representation theory

examples.name: Rank-two valuated matroid from a 2-by-4 matrix
examples.note: A rational 2-by-4 matrix with 2-adic valuations of its Plücker minors makes the definition of a valuated matroid and its tropical linear space explicit.
examples.engagement_reason: he forms the rank-two valuated matroid from the 2-by-4 matrix by taking column determinants, applying the 2-adic valuation, and writing the resulting tropical equations

passes.reason: The rank-two valuated matroid from the 2-by-4 matrix is a small representable example used to illustrate the definition.

examples.name: Three-point modular Golomb-ruler subset
examples.note: A singleton distinct-difference system in a cyclic group, given by a three-point subset with all differences distinct, illustrates the new distinct-difference-system condition.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the three-point subset, examines its possible differences, and gives conditions ensuring the modular Golomb-ruler property

passes.reason: The modular Golomb-ruler condition makes all pairwise modular differences distinct, attaining maximal difference diversity for the subset size.
